texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions 11

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: Use the following safety guidelines to help ensure your own personal safety and to help protect your computer and working environment from potential damage.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Do not attempt to service the computer yourself unless you are a trained service technician. Always follow installation instructions closely.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • If you use an extension power cable with your AC adapter, ensure that the total ampere rating of the products plugged in to the extension power cable does not exceed the ampere rating of the extension cable.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Do not push objects into air vents or openings of your computer. Doing so can cause fire or electric shock by shorting out interior components.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Do not store your computer in a low-airflow environment, such as a carrying case or a closed briefcase, while the computer is turned on. Restricting airflow can damage the computer or cause a fire.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Keep your computer away from radiators and heat sources. Also, do not block cooling vents. Avoid placing loose papers underneath your computer; do not place your computer in a closed-in wall unit or on a bed, sofa, or rug.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Place the AC adapter in a ventilated area, such as a desk top or on the floor, when you use it to run the computer or to charge the battery. Do not cover the AC adapter with papers or other items that will reduce cooling; also, do not use the AC adapter inside a carrying case.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • The AC adapter may become hot during normal operation of your computer. Use care when handling the adapter during or immediately after operation.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Do not allow your portable computer to operate with the base resting directly on exposed skin for extended periods of time. The surface temperature of the base will rise during normal operation (particularly when AC power is present). Allowing sustained contact with exposed skin can cause discomfort or, eventually, a burn.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Do not use your computer in a wet environment, for example, near a bath tub, sink, or swimming pool or in a wet basement.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • If your computer includes an integrated or optional (PC Card) modem, disconnect the modem cable if an electrical storm is approaching to avoid the remote risk of electric shock from lightning via the telephone line.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: 12 CAUTION: Safety Instructions

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • To help avoid the potential hazard of electric shock, do not connect or disconnect any cables or perform maintenance or reconfiguration of this product during an electrical storm. Do not use your computer during an electrical storm unless all cables have been disconnected and the computer is operating on battery power.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • If your computer includes a modem, the cable used with the modem should be manufactured with a minimum wire size of 26 American wire gauge (AWG) and an FCC-compliant RJ-11 modular plug.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Before you open the memory module cover or Mini PCI card/modem cover on the bottom of your computer, disconnect all cables from their electrical outlets and disconnect the telephone cable.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • If your computer has both a modem RJ-11 connector and a network RJ-45 connector, which look alike, make sure you insert the telephone cable into the RJ-11 connector, not the RJ-45 connector.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • PC Cards may become very warm during normal operation. Use care when removing PC Cards after their continuous operation.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Before you clean your computer, disconnect the computer from the electrical outlet. Clean your computer with a soft cloth dampened with water. Do not use liquid or aerosol cleaners, which may contain flammable substances.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Use only the Dell-provided AC adapter approved for use with this computer. Use of another AC adapter may cause a fire or explosion.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • Before you connect the computer to an electrical outlet, check the AC adapter voltage rating to ensure that the required voltage and frequency match the available power source.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • To remove the computer from all power sources, turn the computer off, disconnect the AC adapter from the electrical outlet, and remove any battery installed in the battery bay or module bay.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions
texts.content: • To help prevent electric shock, plug the AC adapter and device power cables into properly grounded power sources. These power cables may be equipped with 3-prong plugs to provide an earth grounding connection. Do not use adapter plugs or remove the grounding prong from the power cable plug. If you use a power extension cable, use the appropriate type, 2-prong or 3-prong, to mate with the AC adapter power cable.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions 13

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Be sure that nothing rests on your AC adapter’s power cable and that the cable is not located where it can be tripped over or stepped on.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • If you are using a multiple-outlet power strip, use caution when plugging the AC adapter’s power cable into the power strip. Some power strips may allow you to insert the plug incorrectly. Incorrect insertion of the power plug could result in permanent damage to your computer, as well as risk of electric shock and/or fire. Ensure that the ground prong of the power plug is inserted into the mating ground contact of the power strip.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Use only Dell™ battery modules that are approved for use with this computer. Use of other types may increase the risk of fire or explosion.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Do not carry a battery pack in your pocket, purse, or other container where metal objects (such as car keys or paper clips) could short-circuit the battery terminals. The resulting excessive current flow can cause extremely high temperatures and may result in damage to the battery pack or cause fire or burns.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • The battery poses a burn hazard if you handle it improperly. Do not disassemble it. Handle a damaged or leaking battery pack with extreme care. If the battery is damaged, electrolyte may leak from the cells and may cause personal injury.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Keep the battery away from children.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Do not store or leave your computer or battery pack near a heat source such as a radiator, fireplace, stove, electric heater, or other heat-generating appliance or otherwise expose it to temperatures in excess of 60ºC (140ºF). When heated to excessive temperatures, battery cells could explode or vent, posing a risk of fire.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Do not dispose of your computer’s battery in a fire or with normal household waste. Battery cells may explode. Discard a used battery according to the manufacturer’s instructions or contact your local waste disposal agency for disposal instructions. Dispose of a spent or damaged battery promptly.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: 14 CAUTION: Safety Instructions

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: • Certain Federal Aviation Administration regulations and/or airline-specific restrictions may apply to the operation of your Dell computer while you are on board an aircraft. For example, such regulations/restrictions may prohibit the use of any personal electronic device (PED) that has the capacity for intentional transmission of radio frequency or other electromagnetic signals while on an aircraft.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: – In order to best comply with all such restrictions, if your Dell portable computer is equipped with Dell TrueMobile™ or some other wireless communication device, please disable this device before you board the aircraft and follow all instructions provided by airline personnel with regard to such device.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: – Additionally, the use of any PED, such as a portable computer, may be prohibited in aircraft during certain critical phases of flight, for example, takeoff and landing. Some airlines may further define the critical flight phase as any time the aircraft is below 3050 m (10,000 ft). Please follow the airline’s specific instructions as to when the use of a PED is allowed.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: Use shielded signal cables to ensure that you maintain the appropriate electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) classification for the intended environment. For parallel printers, a cable is available from Dell. If you prefer, you can order a cable from Dell at its worldwide website at www.dell.com .

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: Static electricity can harm electronic components inside your computer. To prevent static damage, discharge static electricity from your body before you touch any of your computer’s electronic components, such as a memory module. You can do so by touching an unpainted metal surface on the computer’s input/output panel.

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: WARNING: Handling the cord on this product, or cords associated with accessories sold with this product, will expose you to lead, a chemical known to the State of California to cause birth defects or other reproductive harm. Wash your hands after handling the cord .

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)

texts.section_name: CAUTION: Safety Instructions (continued)
texts.content: When Using Your Computer 15

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: When Using Your Computer

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: Observe the following safe-handling guidelines to prevent damage to your computer:

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When setting up the computer for work, place it on a level surface.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When traveling, do not check the computer as baggage. You can put your computer through an X-ray security machine, but never put your computer through a metal detector. If you have the computer checked by hand, be sure to have a charged battery available in case you are asked to turn on the computer.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When traveling with the hard drive removed from the computer, wrap the drive in a nonconducting material, such as cloth or paper. If you have the drive checked by hand, be ready to install the drive in the computer. You can put the hard drive through an X-ray security machine, but never put the drive through a metal detector.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When traveling, do not place the computer in overhead storage compartments where it could slide around. Do not drop your computer or subject it to other mechanical shocks.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • Protect your computer, battery, and hard drive from environmental hazards such as dirt, dust, food, liquids, temperature extremes, and overexposure to sunlight.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When you move your computer between environments with very different temperature and/or humidity ranges, condensation may form on or within the computer. To avoid damaging the computer, allow sufficient time for the moisture to evaporate before using the computer.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: NOTICE: When taking the computer from low-temperature conditions into a warmer environment or from high-temperature conditions into a cooler environment, allow the computer to acclimate to room temperature before turning on power.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When you disconnect a cable, pull on its connector or on its strain-relief loop, not on the cable itself. As you pull out the connector, keep it evenly aligned to avoid bending any connector pins. Also, before you connect a cable make sure both connectors are correctly oriented and aligned.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • Handle components with care. Hold a component such as a memory module by its edges, not its pins.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • When preparing to remove a memory module from the system board or disconnect a device from the computer, turn off the computer, disconnect the AC adapter cable, remove any battery installed in the battery bay or module bay, and then wait 5 seconds before proceeding to help avoid possible damage to the system board.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: 16 When Using Your Computer

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • Clean the display with a soft, clean cloth and water. Apply the water to the cloth; then stroke the cloth across the display in one direction, moving from the top of the display to the bottom. Remove moisture from the display quickly and keep the display dry. Long- term exposure to moisture can damage the display. Do not use a commercial window cleaner to clean your display.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: • If your computer gets wet or is damaged, follow the procedures described in “If Your Computer Gets Wet” on page 87 or "If You Drop or Damage Your Computer" on page 88. If, after following these procedures, you confirm that your computer is not operating properly, contact Dell (see page 126 for the appropriate contact information).

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: Ergonomic Computing Habits

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: CAUTION: Improper or prolonged keyboard use may result in injury.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: CAUTION: Viewing the display or external monitor screen for extended periods of time may result in eye strain.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: For comfort and efficiency, observe the ergonomic guidelines in the Tell Me How help file when setting up and using your computer. To access the help file, see page 80.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: This portable computer is not designed for continuous operation as office equipment. For extended use in an office, it is recommended that you connect an external keyboard.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: When Working Inside Your Computer

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: Before removing or installing memory modules, Mini PCI cards, or modems, perform the following steps in the sequence indicated.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: NOTICE: The only time you should ever access the inside of your computer is when you are installing memory modules, a Mini PCI card, or a modem.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: NOTICE: Wait 5 seconds after turning off the computer before disconnecting a device or removing a memory module, Mini PCI card, or modem to help prevent possible damage to the system board.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: 1 Shut down your computer and turn off any attached devices.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: 2 Disconnect your computer and devices from electrical outlets to reduce the potential for personal injury or shock. Also, disconnect any telephone or telecommunication lines from the computer.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer
texts.content: 3 Remove the main battery from the battery bay and, if necessary, the second battery from the module bay.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: When Using Your Computer (continued)

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: When Using Your Computer 17

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: 4 Ground yourself by touching any unpainted metal surface on the back of the computer.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: While you work, periodically touch the unpainted metal surface to dissipate any static electricity that might harm internal components.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: Protecting Against Electrostatic Discharge

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: Static electricity can harm electronic components inside your computer. To prevent static damage, discharge static electricity from your body before you touch any of your computer’s electronic components, such as a memory module. You can do so by touching any unpainted metal surface on the back of the computer.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: As you continue to work inside the computer, periodically touch any unpainted metal surface on the back of the computer to remove any static charge your body may have accumulated.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: You can also take the following steps to prevent damage from electrostatic discharge (ESD):

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: • When unpacking a static-sensitive component from its shipping carton, do not remove the component from the antistatic packing material until you are ready to install the component. Just before unwrapping the antistatic packaging, be sure to discharge static electricity from your body.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: • When transporting a sensitive component, first place it in an antistatic container or packaging.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: • Handle all sensitive components in a static-safe area. If possible, use antistatic floor pads and workbench pads.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: Your computer uses a lithium-ion battery and a reserve battery. For instructions about replacing the lithium-ion battery in your computer, see "Removing a Battery" on page 44. The reserve battery is a long-life battery, and it is very possible that you will never need to replace it. However, should you need to replace it, the procedure must be performed by an authorized service technician.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: Do not dispose of the battery along with household waste. Contact your local waste disposal agency for the address of the nearest battery deposit site.

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: When Using Your Computer (continued)

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)
texts.content: 18 When Using Your Computer

texts.section_name: When Using Your Computer (continued)

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Use only the AC adapter that came with your computer
texts.content: The AC adapter converts AC power to the DC power required by the computer. You can connect the AC adapter with your computer turned either on or off.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Use only the AC adapter that came with your computer
texts.content: CAUTION: The AC adapter works with electrical outlets worldwide. However, power connectors and power strips vary among countries. Using an incompatible cable or improperly connecting the cable to the power strip or electrical outlet may cause fire or equipment damage.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Use only the AC adapter that came with your computer
texts.content: CAUTION: Place the AC adapter in a ventilated area, such as a desk top or on the floor, when you use it to run the computer or to charge the battery. Do not cover the AC adapter with papers or other items that will reduce cooling; also, do not use the AC adapter inside a carrying case.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Use only the AC adapter that came with your computer
texts.content: NOTICE: When you disconnect the AC adapter cable from the computer, grasp the connector, not the cable itself, and pull firmly but gently to avoid damaging the cable.

texts.content: Dell QuickSet runs from the icon located in the taskbar and functions differently when you click, double-click, or right-click the icon.

texts.section_name: Dell™ QuickSet Features
texts.content: Clicking the QuickSet Icon

texts.section_name: Dell™ QuickSet Features
texts.content: Click the icon to perform the following tasks:

texts.section_name: Dell™ QuickSet Features
texts.content: • Adjust power management settings using the Power Management Wizard.

texts.section_name: Dell™ QuickSet Features
texts.content: • Adjust the size of icons and toolbars.

texts.section_name: Dell™ QuickSet Features

texts.section_name: Connecting to the Internet
texts.content: Connecting to the Internet

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: To connect to the Internet, you need a modem or network connection and an Internet service provider (ISP), such as AOL or MSN. Your ISP will offer one or more of the following Internet connection options:

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: • Dial-up connections that provide Internet access through a phone line. Dial-up connections are considerably slower than DSL and cable modem connections.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: • DSL connections that provide high-speed Internet access through your existing phone line. With a DSL connection, you can access the Internet and use your phone on the same line simultaneously.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: • Cable modem connections that provide high-speed Internet access through your local cable TV line.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: If you are using a dial-up connection, connect a telephone line to the modem connector on your computer and to the telephone wall jack before you set up your Internet connection. If you are using a DSL or cable modem connection, contact your ISP for setup instructions.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: 34 Setting Up Your Computer

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.content: 2 Double-click the MSN Explorer or AOL icon on the Windows ® desktop.

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: • If you have a CD, click Use the CD I got from an ISP .

texts.section_name: NOTE: ISPs and ISP offerings vary by country
texts.content: If you selected Set up my connection manually , continue to step 6. Otherwise, follow the instructions on the screen to complete the setup.

texts.content: NOTE: If you do not know which type of connection to select, contact your ISP.

texts.section_name: NOTE: If you do not know which type of connection to select, contact your ISP
texts.content: 6 Click the appropriate option under How do you want to connect to the Internet? , and then click Next .

texts.section_name: NOTE: If you do not know which type of connection to select, contact your ISP
texts.content: 7 Use the setup information provided by your ISP to complete the setup.

texts.section_name: NOTE: If you do not know which type of connection to select, contact your ISP
texts.content: If you are having problems connecting to the Internet, see "Modem and Internet Connection Problems." If you cannot connect to the Internet but have successfully connected in the past, the ISP might have a service outage. Contact your ISP to check the service status, or try connecting again later.

texts.section_name: Modem and Internet Connection Problems
texts.content: Modem and Internet Connection Problems

texts.section_name: Modem and Internet Connection Problems
texts.content: NOTICE: Connect the modem to an analog telephone wall jack only. Connecting the modem to a digital telephone network damages the modem.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line...
texts.content: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line into the network connector.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line...
texts.content: NOTE: If you can connect to your Internet service provider (ISP), your modem is functioning properly. If you are sure that your modem is working properly and you still experience problems, contact your ISP.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line...
texts.content: C HECK THE TELEPHONE WALL JACK — Disconnect the telephone line from the modem and connect it to a telephone. Listen for a dial tone. Ensure that you have touchtone telephone service. Try connecting the modem to a different telephone wall jack.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line...
texts.content: Slow connection speeds can be caused by telephone noise as well as by telephone line or network conditions. Contact your telephone company or network administrator for more information.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Modem and network connectors look similar. Do not plug a telephone line...
texts.content: C ONNECT THE MODEM DIRECTLY TO THE TELEPHONE WALL JACK — If you have other telephone devices sharing the line, such as an answering machine, fax machine, surge protector, or line splitter, then bypass them and use the telephone line to connect the modem directly to the telephone wall jack.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: C HECK THE TELEPHONE LINE — Try using a different telephone line. If you are using a line that is 3 m (10 ft) or more in length, try a shorter one.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: I RREGULAR DIAL TONE — If you have voice mail service, you might hear an irregular dial tone when you have messages. Contact your telephone company for instructions on restoring a dial tone.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: T URN OFF CALL WAITING ( CATCH - PHONE ) — See your telephone directory for instructions on deactivating this feature. Then adjust the dial-up networking connection properties.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button and click Control Panel .

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 2 Click Printers and Other Hardware , click Phone and Modem Options , click the Dialing Rules tab, and then click Edit... .

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 3 In the Edit Location window, ensure that To disable call waiting, dial: is checked, and then select the proper code as listed in your telephone directory.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 4 Click Apply and click OK .

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 5 Close the Phone and Modems Options window.

texts.section_name: C HECK THE CONNECTION — Verify that the telephone line is connected to the modem
texts.content: 6 Close the Control Panel window.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: Transferring Information to a New Computer

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: The Microsoft ® Windows ® XP operating system provides a Files and Settings Transfer wizard to move data from the source computer to the new computer. You can move data such as:

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: • Internet bookmarks

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: You can transfer the data to the new computer over a network connection, or you can store it on a removable medium, such as a writable CD or floppy disk.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: To prepare the new computer for the file transfer:

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button, point to All Programs → Accessories → System Tools , and then click Files and Settings Transfer Wizard .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 2 When the Files and Settings Transfer Wizard welcome screen appears, click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 3 On the Which computer is this? screen, click New Computer and click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 4 On the Do you have a Windows XP CD? screen, click I will use the wizard from the Windows XP CD and click Next.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 5 When the Now go to your old computer screen appears, go to your old or source computer. Do not click Next at this time.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: To copy data from the old computer:

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 1 On the old computer, insert the Windows XP Operating System CD.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 2 On the Welcome to Microsoft Windows XP screen, click Perform additional tasks .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: Setting Up Your Computer 37

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 3 Under What do you want to do? , click Transfer files and settings .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 4 On the Files and Settings Transfer Wizard welcome screen, click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 5 On the Which computer is this? screen, click Old Computer and click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 6 On the Select a transfer method screen, click the transfer method you prefer.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 7 On the What do you want to transfer? screen, select the items you want to transfer and click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: After the information has been copied, the Completing the Collection Phase screen appears.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: To transfer data to the new computer:

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 1 On the Now go to your old computer screen on the new computer, click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 2 On the Where are the files and settings? screen, select the method you chose for transferring your settings and files and click Next .

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: The wizard reads the collected files and settings and applies them to your new computer.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: When all of the settings and files have been applied, the Finished screen appears.

texts.section_name: Transferring Information to a New Computer
texts.content: 3 Click Finished and restart the new computer.

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: Power Protection Devices

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: Several devices are available to protect against power fluctuations and failures:

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: • Uninterruptible power supplies (UPS)

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: Surge protectors and power strips equipped with surge protection help to prevent damage to your computer from voltage spikes that can occur during electrical storms or following power interruptions. The level of protection is usually commensurate with the cost of the surge protector. Some surge protector manufacturers include warranty coverage for certain types of damage. Carefully read the device warranty when choosing a surge protector. A device with a higher joule rating offers more protection. Compare joule ratings to determine the relative effectiveness of different devices.

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: NOTICE: Most surge protectors do not protect against power fluctuations or power interruptions caused by nearby lightning strikes. When lightning occurs in your area, disconnect the telephone line from the telephone wall jack and disconnect your computer from the electrical outlet.

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: Many surge protectors have a telephone jack for modem protection. See the surge protector documentation for modem connection instructions.

texts.section_name: Power Protection Devices
texts.content: NOTICE: Not all surge protectors offer network adapter protection. Disconnect the network cable from the network wall jack during electrical storms.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Line conditioners do not protect against power interruptions
texts.content: NOTICE: Line conditioners do not protect against power interruptions.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Line conditioners do not protect against power interruptions
texts.content: Line conditioners are designed to maintain AC voltage at a fairly constant level.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Line conditioners do not protect against power interruptions
texts.content: 40 Setting Up Your Computer

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Line conditioners do not protect against power interruptions

texts.content: Uninterruptible Power Supplies

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Loss of power while data is being saved to the hard drive may result in...
texts.content: NOTICE: Loss of power while data is being saved to the hard drive may result in data loss or file damage.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Loss of power while data is being saved to the hard drive may result in...
texts.content: NOTE: To ensure maximum battery operating time, connect only your computer to a UPS. Connect other devices, such as a printer, to a separate power strip that provides surge protection.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Loss of power while data is being saved to the hard drive may result in...
texts.content: A UPS protects against power fluctuations and interruptions. UPS devices contain a battery that provides temporary power to connected devices when AC power is interrupted. The battery charges while AC power is available. See the UPS manufacturer documentation for information on battery operating time and to ensure that the device is approved by Underwriters Laboratories (UL).

texts.section_name: Battery Performance
texts.content: Battery Performance

texts.section_name: Battery Performance
texts.content: NOTE: Batteries for portable computers are covered only during the initial one-year period of the limited warranty for your computer. For more information about the Dell warranty for your computer, see page 145.

texts.section_name: Battery Performance
texts.content: Use a battery to power the computer when it is not connected to an electrical outlet. One battery is supplied as standard equipment in the battery bay.

texts.section_name: Battery Performance
texts.content: Battery operating time varies depending on operating conditions.

texts.section_name: Battery Performance
texts.content: Operating time may be significantly reduced when you perform operations including, but not limited to, the following:

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet when writing to a CD.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: • Using optical drives, especially DVD and CD-RW drives.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: • Using wireless communications devices, PC Cards, or USB devices.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: • Using high-brightness display settings.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: • Using 3D screen savers or other power-intensive programs such as 3D games and engineering programs.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: • Running the computer in maximum performance mode. See "Power Management" in the Tell Me How help file for more information. To access the help file, see page 80.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: You can set power management options to alert you when the battery charge is low. You can also set power modes to optimize power consumption and battery charge time. See page 124 for additional information.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: CAUTION: Using an incompatible battery may increase the risk of fire or explosion. Replace the battery only with a compatible battery purchased from Dell. The lithium-ion battery is designed to work with your Dell™ computer. Do not use a battery from other computers with your computer.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: CAUTION: Do not dispose of batteries with household waste. When your battery no longer holds a charge, call your local waste disposal or environmental agency for advice on disposing of a lithium-ion battery. See "Battery Disposal" on page 17.

texts.section_name: NOTE: It is recommended that you connect your computer to an electrical outlet...
texts.content: CAUTION: Misuse of the battery may increase the risk of fire or chemical burn. Do not puncture, incinerate, disassemble, or expose the battery to temperatures above 65°C (149°F). Keep the battery away from children. Handle damaged or leaking batteries with extreme care. Damaged batteries may leak and cause personal injury or equipment damage.

texts.content: The Windows power meter indicates the remaining battery charge. To check the power

texts.content: tab, see "Power Management" in the Tell Me How help file. To access the help file, see page 80.

texts.section_name: Checking the Battery Charge
texts.content: If the computer is connected to an electrical outlet, a icon appears.

texts.section_name: Checking the Battery Charge
texts.content: NOTICE: To avoid losing or corrupting data, save your work immediately after a low-battery warning. Then connect the computer to an electrical outlet. If the battery runs completely out of power, hibernate mode begins automatically.

texts.section_name: Checking the Battery Charge
texts.content: A pop-up window warns you when the battery charge is approximately 90 percent depleted. For more information on low-battery alarms, see "Power Management" in the Tell Me How help file. To access the help file, see page 80.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: Charging the Battery

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: NOTE: The AC adapter charges a completely discharged battery in approximately 3 hours with the computer turned off. Charge time is significantly longer if the computer is on and running at high processor speeds and system activity levels. Running the computer at high system activity levels for extended periods of time may prevent the battery from charging.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: When you connect the computer to an electrical outlet or install a battery while the computer is connected to an electrical outlet, the computer checks the battery charge and temperature. If necessary, the AC adapter then charges the battery and maintains the battery charge.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: If the battery is hot from being used in your computer or being in a hot environment, the battery may not charge when you connect the computer to an electrical outlet.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: The battery is too hot to start charging if the light flashes alternately green and orange. Disconnect the computer from the electrical outlet and allow the computer and the battery to cool to room temperature. Then connect the computer to an electrical outlet to continue charging the battery.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: NOTE: You can leave the battery in the computer as long as you like. The battery internal circuitry prevents the battery from overcharging.

texts.section_name: Charging the Battery
texts.content: For more information on resolving problems with a battery, see "Power Management" in the Tell Me How help file. To access the help file, see page 80.

texts.content: Removing a Battery

texts.section_name: Removing a Battery
texts.content: CAUTION: Before performing these procedures, disconnect the modem from the telephone wall jack.

texts.section_name: Removing a Battery

texts.section_name: Removing a Battery
texts.content: 3 Remove the battery.

texts.section_name: Installing a Battery
texts.content: Installing a Battery

texts.section_name: Installing a Battery
texts.content: Slide the battery into the battery bay until the latch clicks.

texts.section_name: Storing a Battery
texts.content: Storing a Battery

texts.section_name: Storing a Battery
texts.content: Remove the battery when you store your computer for an extended period of time. A battery discharges during prolonged storage. After a long storage period, recharge the battery fully before you use it.

texts.content: C HECK THE POWER LIGHT — When the power light is lit or blinking, the computer has power. If the power light is blinking, the computer is in standby mode—press the power button to exit standby mode. If the light is off, press the power button to turn on the computer or exit a power management mode. If the computer does not turn on, press and hold the power button for several seconds.

texts.section_name: C HARGE THE BATTERY — The battery charge may be depleted
texts.content: CHECK THE BATTERY STATUS LIGHT — If the battery status light flashes orange or is a steady orange, the battery charge is low or depleted. Connect the computer to an electrical outlet.

texts.section_name: C HARGE THE BATTERY — The battery charge may be depleted
texts.content: If the battery status light flashes green and orange, the battery is too hot to charge. Turn off the computer (see page 40), disconnect the computer from the electrical outlet, and then let the battery and computer cool to room temperature.

texts.section_name: C HARGE THE BATTERY — The battery charge may be depleted
texts.content: If the battery status light rapidly flashes orange, the battery may be defective. Contact Dell (see page 126).

texts.section_name: C HARGE THE BATTERY — The battery charge may be depleted
texts.content: T EST THE ELECTRICAL OUTLET — Ensure that the electrical outlet is working by testing it with another device, such as a lamp.

texts.content: C ONNECT THE COMPUTER DIRECTLY TO AN ELECTRICAL OUTLET — Bypass power protection devices, power strips, and the extension cable to verify that the computer turns on.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: Touch Pad

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: The touch pad detects the pressure and movement of your finger to allow you to move the cursor on the display. Use the touch pad and touch pad buttons as you would use a mouse.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: • To move the cursor, lightly slide your finger over the touch pad.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: • To select an object, lightly tap once on the surface of the touch pad or use your thumb to press the left touch-pad button.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: • To select and move (or drag) an object, position the cursor on the object and tap down-up-down on the touch pad. On the second down motion, leave your finger on the touch pad and move the selected object by sliding your finger over the surface.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: • To double-click an object, position the cursor on the object and tap twice on the touch pad or use your thumb to press the left touch-pad button twice.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: Customizing the Touch Pad

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: You can use the Mouse Properties window to disable the touch pad or adjust its settings.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button, click Control Panel , and then click Printers and Other Hardware .

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: 3 In the Mouse Properties window, click the Device Settings tab.

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: To disable the touch pad or mouse, highlight the device and click Disable .

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: To change the touch pad or mouse settings, highlight the device, click Settings , select the desired setting(s), and then click Apply .

texts.section_name: Touch Pad
texts.content: 4 Click OK to save the settings and close the window, and then click OK again to exit the Mouse Properties window.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: Connecting a Television to the Computer

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: Your computer has an S-video TV-out connector that enables you to connect the computer to a television. Using a commercially available S-video cable or composite video cable, you can connect the computer to a television in one of two ways:

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: NOTE: Video and audio cables for connecting your computer to a television are not included with your computer. Cables may be purchased at most consumer electronics stores.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: • S-video (for a television with S-video input)

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: • Composite video (for a television with only a composite video input; also uses a composite TV-out adapter cable)

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: The audio connector on the side of the computer enables you to connect the computer to your television or audio device, using a commercially available audio cable.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: NOTE: Diagrams for each connection combination appear at the beginning of each subsection to help you determine which method you should use.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: When you complete the cable connection, see "Enabling the Display Settings for a Television" on page 62 to ensure that the computer recognizes and works properly with the television.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: Using CDs, DVDs, and Other Multimedia 59

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: Before you begin, ensure that you have the following cables:

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 1 Turn off the computer and the television and/or audio device you want to connect.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 2 Plug one end of the S-video cable into the S-video connector on the computer.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 3 Plug the other end of the S-video cable into your television.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 4 Plug the single-connector end of the audio cable into the headphone connector on your computer.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 60 Using CDs, DVDs, and Other Multimedia

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 5 Plug the two RCA connectors on the other end of the audio cable into the audio input connectors on your television or other audio device.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 6 Turn on the television, turn on any audio device you connected, and then turn on the computer.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 7 See "Enabling the Display Settings for a Television" on page 62 to ensure that the computer recognizes and works properly with the television.

texts.content: Composite Video Connection

texts.content: To connect the computer to a television that has a composite video input only, use a composite TV-out adapter cable. Before you begin, ensure that you have the following cables:

texts.content: 1 Turn off the computer and the television and/or audio device you want to connect.

texts.section_name: Connecting a Television to the Computer
texts.content: 2 Connect the composite TV-out adapter cable to the S-video TV-out connector on the computer.

texts.section_name: Connecting to a Network Adapter
texts.content: Connecting to a Network Adapter

texts.section_name: Connecting to a Network Adapter
texts.content: Before you connect your computer to a network, the computer must have a network adapter installed and a network cable connected to it.

texts.section_name: Connecting to a Network Adapter
texts.content: To connect a network cable:

texts.section_name: Connecting to a Network Adapter
texts.content: 1 Connect the network cable to the network adapter connector on your computer.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Insert the cable until it clicks into place, and then gently pull it to...
texts.content: NOTE: Insert the cable until it clicks into place, and then gently pull it to ensure that it is secure.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Insert the cable until it clicks into place, and then gently pull it to...
texts.content: 2 Connect the other end of the network cable to a network connection device, such as a network wall jack.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Do not use a network cable with a telephone wall jack
texts.content: NOTE: Do not use a network cable with a telephone wall jack.

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: Network Setup Wizard

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: The Microsoft ® Windows ® XP operating system provides a Network Setup Wizard to guide you through the process of sharing files, printers, or an Internet connection between computers in a home or small office.

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button, point to All Programs → Accessories → Communications , and then click Network Setup Wizard .

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: Setting Up a Home and Office Network 67

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: 2 On the welcome screen, click Next .

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: 3 Click Checklist for creating a network .

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: NOTE: Selecting the connection method This computer connects directly to the Internet enables the integrated firewall provided with Windows XP.

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: 4 Complete the checklist and required preparations, and return to the Network Setup Wizard.

texts.section_name: Network Setup Wizard
texts.content: 5 Follow the instructions on the screen.

texts.section_name: Network Problems
texts.content: C HECK THE NETWORK CABLE CONNECTOR — Ensure that the network cable connector is firmly connected to the connector on the computer and the network wall jack.

texts.section_name: Network Problems
texts.content: C HECK THE NETWORK LIGHTS ON THE NETWORK CONNECTOR — Green indicates that the network connection is active. If the status light is not green, try replacing the network cable. Amber indicates that the network adapter driver is loaded and the adapter is detecting activity.

texts.section_name: R ESTART THE COMPUTER — Try to log on to the network again
texts.content: R ESTART THE COMPUTER — Try to log on to the network again.

texts.section_name: R ESTART THE COMPUTER — Try to log on to the network again
texts.content: C ONTACT YOUR NETWORK ADMINISTRATOR — Verify that your network settings are correct and that the network is functioning.

texts.content: Connecting to Wireless Local Area Networks

texts.section_name: Connecting to Wireless Local Area Networks
texts.content: Before you can connect to a wireless Local Area Network (LAN), you must have specific information about your network. Be sure to get the name of your wireless network along with any special security settings from your network administrator. These settings are unique to your network and cannot be provided by Dell.

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...

texts.content: Determining Your Network Type

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...
texts.content: Wireless networks fall into two categories — infrastructure networks and ad-hoc networks. An infrastructure network uses routers or access points to connect several computers together. An ad-hoc network does not use routers or access points and consists of computers that broadcast to one another.

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...
texts.content: NOTE: Most wireless networks are the infrastructure type. Consult your network administrator if you are unsure of the type of network to which you are connecting.

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...
texts.content: Connecting to a Network in Windows XP

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...
texts.content: Your wireless network card requires proper software and drivers in order to connect to a network. This software is pre-installed from the factory. If the software is removed or corrupted, follow the instructions listed in the User's Guide for your wireless card. The User’s Guide is located on your Drivers, Utilities, and Applications CD (that came with your computer) in the "User’s Guides-Network User’s Guides" category. The User’s Guide is also available on the Dell Support website at support.dell.com .

texts.section_name: NOTE: These networking instructions do not apply to Bluetooth™ or cellular...

texts.content: Connecting to a Network With Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) Security Requirements

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: The following instructions are basic steps for connecting to a WPA network. If your network requires a user name, password, or domain-settings, consult the setup instructions in the User’s Guide for your wireless network card.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: NOTE: WPA protocols require that you know the Network Authentication and Data Encryption settings for your wireless network. In addition, your WPA-protected network may require special settings such as a network key, user name, password, and domain name. Be sure to get all necessary WPA settings from your network administrator before continuing.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: 1 In the Preferred networks section, click the name of your wireless network.

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: 3 From the Network Authentication drop-down menu, select your network authentication type (as provided by the network administrator).

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: If you do not see the drop-down menus, you must update your wireless software before continuing. Download and install the latest version of your wireless driver software from the Dell Support website at support.dell.com .

texts.section_name: NOTE: Your computer may take up to a minute to attach to the network
texts.content: 5 If your wireless network requires a key, enter it in the Network key field.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: If Your Computer Gets Wet

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: CAUTION: Perform this procedure only after you are certain that it is safe to do so. If the computer is connected to an electrical outlet, it is recommended that you turn off AC power at the circuit breaker before attempting to remove the power cables from the electrical outlet. Use the utmost caution when removing wet cables from a live power source.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 1 Shut down the computer (see page 40), disconnect the AC adapter from the computer, and then disconnect the AC adapter from the electrical outlet.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 2 Turn off any attached external devices, and disconnect them from their power sources and then from the computer.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 3 Ground yourself by touching one of the metal connectors on the back of the computer.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 4 Remove any installed PC Cards, and put them in a safe place to dry.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 5 Remove the battery.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 6 Wipe off the battery and put it in a safe place to dry.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 7 Wipe off the display.

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 8 Remove the hard drive (see page 106).

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 9 Remove the memory module(s) (see page 100).

texts.section_name: If Your Computer Gets Wet
texts.content: 10 Open the display and place the computer right-side up across two books or similar props to let air circulate all around it. Let the computer dry for at least 24 hours in a dry area at room temperature.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed the drying process.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: CAUTION: To help prevent electrical shock, verify that the computer is thoroughly dry before continuing with the rest of this procedure.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: 11 Ground yourself by touching one of the metal connectors on the back of the computer.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: 12 Replace the memory module(s), the memory module cover, and the screw(s).

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: 13 Replace the hard drive.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: 14 Replace any PC Cards you removed.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: C ONTACT D ELL — If you cannot solve your problem using the Dell™ Support website or e-mail service, call Dell for technical assistance (see page 126). See "Dell Technical Support Policy (U.S. Only)" on page 125 for a description of the hardware and software support provided by Dell.

texts.content: 15 Replace the battery.

texts.section_name: NOTICE: Do not use artificial means, such as a hair dryer or a fan, to speed...
texts.content: 16 Turn on the computer and verify that it is working properly.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Drivers

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: A driver is a program that controls a device such as a printer, mouse, or keyboard. All devices require a driver program.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: A driver acts like a translator between the device and the programs that use the device. Each device has its own set of specialized commands that only its driver recognizes.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Many drivers such as the keyboard driver come with your Microsoft ® Windows ® operating system. You may need to install drivers if:

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: • You upgrade your operating system.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: • You reinstall your operating system.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: • You connect or install a new device.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If you experience a problem with any device, perform the steps in the following sections to identify whether the driver is the source of your problem and to update the driver, if necessary.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button and click Control Panel .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 2 In the Control Panel window, under Pick a Category , click Performance and Maintenance .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 3 In the Performance and Maintenance window, click System .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 4 In the System Properties screen, click the Hardware tab.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 5 Click Device Manager .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 6 In the Device Manager window, scroll down the list to see if any device has an exclamation point (a yellow circle with a [ !] ) on the device icon.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If an exclamation point is next to the device name, you may need to reinstall the driver or install a new driver.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Reinstalling Drivers

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: NOTICE: The Dell Support website and your Drivers and Utilities CD provide approved drivers for Dell™ computers. If you install drivers obtained from other sources, your computer might not work correctly.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Using Windows XP Device Driver Rollback

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If a problem occurs on your computer after you install or update a driver, use Windows XP Device Driver Rollback to replace the driver with the previously installed version.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: w w w. d e l l . c o m | s u p p o r t . d e l l . c o m

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: To use Device Driver Rollback:

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 1 Click the Start button and click Control Panel .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 2 In the Control Panel window, under Pick a Category , click Performance and Maintenance .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 3 In the Performance and Maintenance window, click System .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 4 In the System Properties screen, click the Hardware tab.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 5 Click Device Manager .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 6 In the Device Manager window, right-click the device for which the new driver was installed, and then click Properties .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 7 Click the Drivers tab.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 8 Click Roll Back Driver .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If Device Driver Rollback does not resolve the problem, then use System Restore (see page 92) to return your operating system to the operating state it was in before you installed the new driver.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Using the Drivers and Utilities CD

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If using Device Driver Rollback or System Restore (see page 92) does not resolve the problem, then reinstall the driver from the Drivers and Utilities CD.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 1 Save and close any open files, and exit any open programs.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 2 Insert the Drivers and Utilities CD.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: In most cases, the CD starts running automatically. If it does not, start Windows Explorer, click your CD drive directory to display the CD contents, and then double- click the autocd.exe file. The first time that you run the CD, it might prompt you to install setup files. Click OK , and follow the instructions on the screen to continue.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 3 From the Language drop-down menu in the toolbar, select your preferred language for the driver or utility (if available).

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: A welcome screen appears.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 4 Click Next . The CD automatically scans your hardware to detect drivers and utilities used by your computer.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: After the CD completes the hardware scan, you can also detect other drivers and utilities. Under Search Criteria , select the appropriate categories from the System Model , Operating System , and Topic drop-down menus.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: A link or links appear(s) for the specific drivers and utilities used by your computer.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 5 Click the link of a specific driver or utility to display information about the driver or utility that you want to install.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 6 Click the Install button (if present) to begin installing the driver or utility. At the welcome screen, follow the screen prompts to complete the installation.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If no Install button is present, automatic installation is not an option. For installation instructions, either see the appropriate instructions in the following subsections, or click Extract , follow the extracting instructions, and read the readme file.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: If instructed to navigate to the driver files, click the CD directory on the driver information window to display the files associated with that driver.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: Manually Reinstalling Drivers for Windows XP

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 1 After extracting the driver files to your hard drive as described in the previous section, click the Start button and right-click My Computer .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 2 Click Properties .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 3 Click the Hardware tab and click Device Manager .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 4 Double-click the type of device for which you are installing the driver (for example, Modems or Infrared devices ).

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 5 Double-click the name of the device for which you are installing the driver.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 6 Click the Driver tab and click Update Driver .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 7 Click Install from a list or specific location (Advanced) and click Next .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 8 Click Browse , and browse to the location to which you previously extracted the driver files.

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 9 When the name of the appropriate driver appears, click Next .

texts.section_name: Drivers
texts.content: 10 Click Finish and restart your computer.

texts.section_name: Drivers
